fair.title: FAIR Data Principles
fair.subtitle: Guidelines for Making Research Data Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, and Reusable
fair.description: FAIR comprises a set of guiding principles to make research data and digital assets Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, and Reusable.

findable.title: Findable
findable.description: Your data becomes easily discoverable through persistent identifiers (DOI, Handle) and rich metadata in search engines and catalogs. This increases your research visibility and opens doors to new collaborations.

accessible.title: Accessible
accessible.description: Secure access to your data is provided through standard protocols like HTTP/HTTPS. Authentication and authorization mechanisms enable controlled sharing. Metadata remains always accessible.

interoperable.title: Interoperable
interoperable.description: Using standard data formats (JSON, XML, CSV) and controlled vocabularies, your data integrates seamlessly with other systems. This facilitates data analysis and meta-analyses.

reusable.title: Reusable
reusable.description: With open licenses like Creative Commons and detailed provenance information, your data can be safely reused by future generations of researchers. This accelerates scientific progress.

hero.title: FAIR Data Principles
hero.subtitle: Maximize your research's global impact by enhancing the findability, accessibility, interoperability, and reusability of your data
hero.description: Adopted by 50,000+ researchers worldwide since 2016, FAIR principles help you increase your data's scientific value, build new collaborations, and ensure the sustainability of your research outcomes

whatIsFair.title: What is FAIR?
whatIsFair.subtitle: Each letter represents a critical principle in data management

benefits.title: Benefits of FAIR Principles
benefits.subtitle: Enhance your research impact by making your data FAIR

items.title: Increase Research Impact
items.description: FAIR data receives 25% more citations and is used in multidisciplinary research. Boost your academic career by increasing your visibility.

items.title: Build Global Collaborations
items.description: Collaborate with researchers worldwide through standardized data. Scientists from 150+ countries can use your FAIR data.

items.title: Create Long-term Value
items.description: Your data remains valuable even 10-20 years later. Transfer your research legacy to future generations with digital preservation strategies.

items.title: Guarantee Data Quality
items.description: 90% fewer data errors with standard metadata schemas. Create error-free datasets with automated validation tools.

items.title: Increase Research Efficiency 3x
items.description: Reduce analysis time with organized data. Instantly find and reuse your past work thanks to metadata.

items.title: Ensure Security and Compliance
items.description: Meet GDPR, international funding agency requirements. Minimize legal risks with secure data sharing.

successStories.subtitle: Real experiences from researchers implementing FAIR principles

statistics.subtitle: Current statistics showing the global impact of FAIR principles

cta.title: Start Your FAIR Journey Today
cta.description: Assess your data's FAIR status in just 15 minutes and maximize your research impact with proven methods used by 50,000+ researchers worldwide
cta.tip: 💡 Tip: The assessment takes only 5-10 minutes and you get instant personalized recommendations

assessment.title: FAIR Principles Assessment
assessment.subtitle: Evaluate your dataset's compliance with FAIR principles using our comprehensive assessment tool. Get personalized recommendations and actionable insights to improve your data management practices.

features.comprehensive: Comprehensive evaluation of all FAIR principles
features.duration: Typical completion time for comprehensive assessment

f1.title: Unique and Persistent Identifiers
f1.question: Does your dataset have a unique and persistent identifier (like DOI, Handle, or UUID)?
f1.explanation: A unique identifier ensures your dataset can be consistently referenced and cited. Persistent identifiers like DOI remain valid even if the location changes.

yes_doi.label: Yes, we use DOI or similar persistent identifier
yes_doi.feedback: Excellent! DOI and similar persistent identifiers are the gold standard for dataset identification.

yes_uuid.label: Yes, we use UUID or similar unique identifier
yes_uuid.feedback: Good! UUIDs provide uniqueness but consider upgrading to persistent identifiers like DOI for better longevity.

partial.label: We have some form of identifier but it's not globally unique
partial.feedback: This is a start, but consider implementing globally unique identifiers for better discoverability.

no.label: No, we don't have unique identifiers
no.feedback: Consider implementing unique identifiers as a priority. Start with DOI or UUID systems.

tips.1: Consider Handle System for institutional repositories
tips.2: Ensure identifiers are included in all metadata

tips.0: Use institutional repositories for internal discoverability
tips.1: Register in disciplinary databases for domain-specific discovery
tips.2: Consider general repositories like Zenodo for broader reach

f2.title: Rich Metadata
f2.question: How comprehensive is the metadata describing your dataset?
f2.explanation: Rich metadata helps both humans and machines understand what your dataset contains, how it was created, and how it can be used.

comprehensive.label: Comprehensive metadata including methodology, variables, and context
comprehensive.feedback: Excellent! Comprehensive metadata is crucial for dataset discovery and reuse.

good.label: Good metadata with basic information and some details
good.feedback: Good start! Consider adding more methodological details and context information.

basic.label: Basic metadata with title, author, and description
basic.feedback: Basic metadata is present but could be enriched with more details about methodology and context.

minimal.label: Minimal or no metadata
minimal.feedback: Metadata is essential for findability. Start with basic elements and gradually add more details.

tips.0: Include creation date, methodology, and data collection details

f3.title: Metadata-Data Relationship
f3.question: Does your metadata clearly identify which dataset it describes?
f3.explanation: Metadata should explicitly reference the dataset it describes, creating a clear connection between the two.

explicit.label: Yes, metadata explicitly references the dataset identifier
explicit.feedback: Perfect! Clear metadata-data relationships are essential for automated discovery.

implicit.label: The relationship is implicit but clear
implicit.feedback: Consider making the relationship more explicit by including dataset identifiers in metadata.

unclear.label: The relationship is unclear or ambiguous
unclear.feedback: Make the metadata-data relationship more explicit to improve automated discovery.

no.label: No clear relationship between metadata and data
no.feedback: Establish clear connections between metadata and data by including explicit references.

f4.title: Searchable Registry
f4.question: Are your data and metadata registered in a searchable resource?
f4.explanation: Registration in searchable catalogs, repositories, or databases makes your dataset discoverable by search engines and other researchers.

multiple.label: Registered in multiple searchable repositories/catalogs
multiple.feedback: Excellent! Multiple registrations increase discoverability across different communities.

institutional.label: Registered in institutional or disciplinary repository
institutional.feedback: Great! Institutional repositories provide good discoverability within your domain.

single.label: Registered in one searchable resource
single.feedback: Good start! Consider registering in additional relevant repositories for broader reach.

no.label: Not registered in any searchable resource
no.feedback: Consider registering your dataset in appropriate repositories to increase discoverability.

tips.0: Use institutional repositories for internal discoverability
tips.1: Register in disciplinary databases for domain-specific discovery
tips.2: Consider general repositories like Zenodo for broader reach

a1.title: Standard Communication Protocol
a1.question: Are your data and metadata accessible via standardized communication protocols?
a1.explanation: Standard protocols such as HTTP, HTTPS, FTP provide universal access to your data.

multiple.label: Multiple standard protocols supported (HTTP/HTTPS, FTP, etc.)
multiple.feedback: Excellent! Multiple protocol support provides maximum accessibility.

https.label: Secure access via HTTPS
https.feedback: Great! HTTPS has become the standard for secure data access.

http.label: Access via HTTP
http.feedback: Good, but consider upgrading to HTTPS for sensitive data.

proprietary.label: Proprietary or non-standard protocol
proprietary.feedback: Consider adopting standard protocols for broader accessibility.

a1_1.title: Open Protocol
a1_1.question: Are the access protocols open, free, and universally implementable?
a1_1.explanation: Open protocols ensure that anyone can access your data without proprietary software or licenses.

fully_open.label: Fully open and free protocols
fully_open.feedback: Excellent! Open protocols provide maximum accessibility.

mostly_open.label: Mostly open with some minor restrictions
mostly_open.feedback: Good! Consider removing remaining restrictions for full openness.

mixed.label: Mix of open and proprietary protocols
mixed.feedback: Consider migrating to open protocols where possible.

proprietary.label: Proprietary or licensed protocols
proprietary.feedback: Consider adopting open protocols to improve accessibility.

a1_2.title: Authentication and Authorization
a1_2.question: Does your access protocol support authentication and authorization when needed?
a1_2.explanation: Secure authentication protects sensitive data while enabling controlled access.

comprehensive.label: Comprehensive authentication and detailed authorization
comprehensive.feedback: Excellent! Good balance between security and accessibility.

standard.label: Standard authentication systems (OAuth, SAML, etc.)
standard.feedback: Great! Standard systems are secure and widely supported.

basic.label: Basic authentication available
basic.feedback: Good start! Consider modern standards for enhanced security.

none.label: No authentication system
none.feedback: Consider adding authentication for sensitive data.

a2.title: Persistent Metadata
a2.question: Will metadata remain accessible even if the data is no longer available?
a2.explanation: Metadata persistence ensures that context and information are preserved even if data is lost.

guaranteed.label: Yes, metadata persistence is guaranteed
guaranteed.feedback: Excellent! Persistent metadata ensures long-term information preservation.

likely.label: Likely, with strong institutional support
likely.feedback: Good! Consider establishing formal persistence policies.

uncertain.label: Unclear, no explicit policy
uncertain.feedback: Develop clear policies for metadata persistence.

no.label: No, metadata will be lost with data
no.feedback: Develop strategies to preserve metadata independently from data.

i1.title: Formal Language
i1.question: Do your data and metadata use a formal, accessible, shared, and broadly applicable language for knowledge representation?
i1.explanation: Standard formats like RDF, JSON-LD, XML enable machine-to-machine interoperability.

multiple_standards.label: Multiple standard formats supported (RDF, JSON-LD, XML)
multiple_standards.feedback: Excellent! Multiple format support provides maximum interoperability.

single_standard.label: Single widely-used standard in use
single_standard.feedback: Good! A widely-used standard provides good interoperability.

semi_standard.label: Semi-standard or community format
semi_standard.feedback: Consider migrating to full standards for broader interoperability.

proprietary.label: Proprietary or non-standard format
proprietary.feedback: Consider adopting standard formats to improve interoperability.

i2.title: FAIR Vocabularies
i2.question: Do your data and metadata use vocabularies that follow FAIR principles?
i2.explanation: FAIR vocabularies enable consistent and machine-understandable use of terms.

extensive.label: Extensive use of FAIR vocabularies
extensive.feedback: Excellent! FAIR vocabularies maximize semantic interoperability.

moderate.label: Some FAIR vocabularies in use
moderate.feedback: Good! Consider integrating more FAIR vocabularies.

minimal.label: Minimal FAIR vocabulary usage
minimal.feedback: Use more standard vocabularies to improve interoperability.

none.label: No FAIR vocabularies used
none.feedback: Research and implement FAIR vocabularies for semantic interoperability.

tips.2: Adopt domain-specific vocabularies like MeSH for medical data

i3.title: Qualified References
i3.question: Do your data and metadata include qualified references to other data and metadata?
i3.explanation: Clear references to other resources create data networks and enrich context.

comprehensive.label: Comprehensive and qualified cross-references
comprehensive.feedback: Excellent! Rich references provide data networks and enhanced discovery.

good.label: Good references to related datasets
good.feedback: Good! Consider adding more references for richer context.

basic.label: Some basic references available
basic.feedback: Add more qualified references to strengthen data context.

none.label: No references to other resources
none.feedback: Consider adding references to related datasets and resources.

r1.title: Rich Description with Attributes
r1.question: Are your data described with a plurality of accurate and relevant attributes?
r1.explanation: Detailed attributes help users understand and properly use your data.

comprehensive.label: Comprehensive description with rich metadata
comprehensive.feedback: Excellent! Rich description provides maximum reusability.

detailed.label: Well-detailed attributes
detailed.feedback: Great! Detailed attributes provide good reusability.

basic.label: Basic attributes available
basic.feedback: Add more descriptive attributes to improve reusability.

minimal.label: Minimal description
minimal.feedback: Add comprehensive attributes to increase your data's value.

r1_1.title: Clear Usage License
r1_1.question: Are your data published with a clear and accessible usage license?
r1_1.explanation: Clear licenses explicitly state how users can use your data.

open.label: Open license (CC0, CC-BY, etc.)
open.feedback: Excellent! Open licenses provide maximum reusability.

clear.label: License with clear usage terms
clear.feedback: Good! Clear licensing enables safe reuse.

restrictive.label: Restrictive but clear license
restrictive.feedback: Consider more open licenses for broader usage.

none.label: No license information
none.feedback: Always add a license for legal clarity.

r1_2.title: Detailed Provenance Information
r1_2.question: Are your data associated with detailed provenance information?
r1_2.explanation: Provenance information documents where data came from and how it was processed, building trust.

complete.label: Complete provenance information and processing history
complete.feedback: Excellent! Detailed provenance provides maximum trust and reusability.

good.label: Well-documented provenance information
good.feedback: Great! Good provenance documentation enables reliable reuse.

basic.label: Basic provenance information available
basic.feedback: Expand provenance documentation for more trust.

none.label: No provenance information
none.feedback: Consider documenting data origin and processing history.

r1_3.title: Community Standards
r1_3.question: Do your data and metadata meet community standards for the relevant domain?
r1_3.explanation: Domain-specific standards enable seamless data sharing and reuse within your community.

certified.label: Certified or fully compliant
certified.feedback: Excellent! Full compliance with community standards provides ideal reusability.

compliant.label: Largely compliant
compliant.feedback: Great! Good standard compliance facilitates community use.

partial.label: Partially compliant
partial.feedback: Complete missing standards for full compliance.

none.label: Community standards not followed
none.feedback: Research and implement standards in your field.

findable.title: Findable
findable.description: The first step in making data reusable is to ensure it can be found by both humans and computers.

accessible.title: Accessible
accessible.description: Data should be accessible through standard protocols with appropriate authorization.

interoperable.title: Interoperable
interoperable.description: Data should be designed to integrate with other data and be usable with various applications.

reusable.title: Reusable
reusable.description: Data should be optimized for future use and prepared for reuse with clear licenses.

footer.description: Enhance the findability, accessibility, interoperability, and reusability of your data with FAIR data principles.
footer.tagline: A better data ecosystem with FAIR data principles.

descriptions.faq: Questions and answers about FAIR data
